writing_systems.script_is_logographic.note: "Chinese characters are logographs...representing the smallest units of meaning called morphemes" — Wikipedia: Chinese character classification / Writing system

writing_systems.simplified_is_standard_m.note: "Simplified Chinese characters are the standard forms used in mainland China, Malaysia, and Singapore" — Wikipedia: Simplified Chinese characters

casing.no_case_distinction.note: "Chinese writing is indeed a unicase writing system—it does not have the distinction between uppercase and lowercase" — Wikipedia: Unicase

writing_direction.modern_standard_horizo.note: "the layout used in Western scripts...became predominant in mainland China, where it was mandated by the Chinese government in 1955" — Wikipedia: Horizontal and vertical writing in East Asian scripts

tone_system.tone_count_standard.note: "four lexical tones (T1, high level; T2, rising; T3, falling-rising; T4, falling) as well as a neutral tone" — Wikipedia: Standard Chinese phonology

tone_system.neutral_tone_exists.note: "Mandarin Chinese also has a so-called neutral or floating tone (T0)" — Wikipedia: Standard Chinese phonology

tone_system.tones_are_phonemic.note: "two phonemic tones are considered contrastive if there exists a minimal pair"; ma = mother/hemp/horse/scold by tone — Wikipedia: Minimal pair / Tone (linguistics)

pinyin_romanization.official_standard.note: "The ISO adopted pinyin as the standard romanization for modern Chinese in 1982 (ISO 7098:1982...)" — Wikipedia: Pinyin

pinyin_romanization.tone_marked_on_vowel.note: "The diacritic is placed over the letter that represents the syllable nucleus" — Wikipedia: Pinyin

grapheme_to_phoneme.character_reading_no.note: "Chinese characters do not represent their pronunciation, making it impossible to predict how to read a character based solely on its shape" — Wikipedia: Chinese character sounds

grapheme_to_phoneme.phonetic_component_s.note: "a semantic component gives a broad category of meaning, while a phonetic component suggests the sound"; but relationship "largely broken down" over time — Wikipedia: Semantic-phonetic compound / Chinese character sounds

phoneme_inventory.syllable_structure_ini.note: "Mandarin Chinese is organized around syllables that are created by an initial followed by a final" — Wikipedia (syllable structure, Mandarin phonology sources)

word_segmentation.no_spaces_between_word.note: "Chinese sentences are traditionally written as strings of characters, with no marks between words" — Wikipedia: Chinese word-segmented writing

word_segmentation.word_boundary_often_am.note: "different segmentations of a given Chinese string can represent very different meanings" (乒乓球拍卖完了 example) — Wikipedia: Chinese word-segmented writing

word_order.basic.note: "Chinese is classified as an SVO (subject–verb–object) language" — Wikipedia: Chinese grammar

word_order.topic_prominent.note: "Chinese can be considered a topic-prominent language: there is a strong preference for sentences that begin with the topic..." — Wikipedia: Chinese grammar

word_order.adjective_before_noun.note: "Modifiers usually precede the word they modify, so that adjectives precede nouns" — Wikipedia: Chinese adjectives

classifiers.required_before_countable_no.note: "When a noun is preceded by a numeral..., the use of a classifier or measure word is in most cases considered mandatory" — Wikipedia: Chinese classifier / Measure word

classifiers.ge_is_general_default.note: "the classifier gè (个/個)...also serves as a general classifier, which may often be used in place of other classifiers" — Wikipedia: Chinese classifier

inflection_morphology.no_verb_conjugatio.note: "Chinese has no tenses, no voices, and no grammatical number"; "Chinese does not have grammatical markers of tense" — Wikipedia: Chinese grammar

inflection_morphology.aspect_marked_by_p.note: "Mandarin Chinese has aspect equipped with aspectual particles (e.g., le, zhe, guo)...signal grammatical aspects" — Wikipedia: Chinese particles / Chinese grammar

pronoun_system.fixed_pronouns_not_kinshi.note: "The singular pronouns in Mandarin are wǒ 我 'I', nǐ 你 'you', nín 您 'you (formal)', and tā..." — Wikipedia: Pronouns in Chinese

pronoun_system.nin_is_polite_you.note: "the second-person singular pronoun 你 (nǐ) can be substituted with its polite form 您 (nín)...to express politeness" — Wikipedia: Pronouns in Chinese

register_taxonomy.mainly_word_choice_and.note: "honorific language in Chinese is achieved by using honorific...alternatives, prefixing/suffixing...or dropping casual-sounding words" vs. Japanese/Korean's elaborate grammatical systems — Wikipedia: Chinese honorifics

sentence_final_particles.ma_marks_yes_no.note: "yes-no questions can be marked with the sentence-final particle 'ma'" — Wikipedia: Sentence-final particle / Chinese grammar

sentence_final_particles.ba_marks_sugges.note: "ba (吧) is used for soliciting approval, softening an imperative...used to form first-person imperatives, equivalent to 'let's...'" — Wikipedia: Sentence-final particle

forms_of_address.xiansheng_nvshi_formal_.note: "Xiānshēng (先生)...can either follow the surname...In modern Standard Chinese, it is also used...as the title 'Mr'"; nǚshì used for women — Wikipedia: Chinese titles

naturalness_translation.word_order_liter.note: "Wrong word order is a characteristic of Chinglish"; Westernised Chinese direct glosses from English "pose comprehension issues" — Wikipedia: Chinglish / Westernised Chinese language
